---
draft: false
title: "Workers' Health under Climate Change: Agrochemicals"
description: "Infographic on agrochemical exposure risk across Brazilian agriculture — scored by pesticide application intensity and toxicity, disaggregated by municipality, population, and crop."
date: 2026-02-06
categories: [Infographic, Climate Change, Workers Health, CLIDEWO]
image: img/agrochemicals_logo_02.png
---

[← Back to Portfolio](../../portfolio.html){.back-link}

::: {.label}
Infographic · Agrochemicals · Brazil · CLIDEWO
:::

Agricultural workers are regularly exposed to agrochemicals — pesticides, herbicides, fertilisers — through skin contact, inhalation, and contaminated food and water. Short-term effects include skin irritation, eye problems, headaches, and respiratory difficulty. Long-term exposure is linked to chronic respiratory disease, hormonal disruption, reproductive problems, nerve damage, and elevated cancer risk. Limited access to protective equipment and training amplifies these risks.

## Agrochemicals score

The agrochemicals score integrates two indicators:

- **Application of pesticides** — sourced from Ormaza-Zulueta & Mehrabi (2025), *Environmental Research Communications*[^1]
- **Pesticides risk score** — sourced from Tang et al. (2021), *Nature Geoscience*[^2]

The visualisation summarises agrochemical exposure risk in **Brazil**, disaggregated by **municipality, population, and crop type**, to identify spatial differences and exposure hotspots. By translating complex climate and socio-environmental data into accessible visual formats, it supports targeted interventions and risk-informed policy.

---

![Workers' health under climate change: Agrochemicals](img/02_viz_agrochemicals.png){width=100%}

---

[Download PDF](img/viz_agrochemicals.pdf)

## About this series

This infographic is part of a nine-piece series on climate hazards and workers' health, developed for the [CLIDEWO](https://www.clidewo.com) project. The series draws on the framework set out in the ILO's *Ensuring Safety and Health at Work in a Changing Climate* (2024).[^3]

[^1]: Ormaza-Zulueta, N. & Mehrabi, Z. (2025). Global inequality in environmental conditions underpinning human rights. *Environmental Research Communications*, 7, 095017. <https://doi.org/10.1088/2515-7620/ae0407>

[^2]: Tang, F.H.M., Lenzen, M., McBratney, A. & Maggi, F. (2021). Risk of pesticide pollution at the global scale. *Nature Geoscience*, 14, 206–210. <https://doi.org/10.1038/s41561-021-00712-5>

[^3]: International Labour Organization (2024). *Ensuring safety and health at work in a changing climate*. <https://www.ilo.org/publications/ensuring-safety-and-health-work-changing-climate>
